digest.editor_note: Fellow Humans, the Middle East just repriced everything. U.S.-Israeli strikes killed Iran's Supreme Leader Khamenei, Tehran is firing back at Gulf states, the Strait of Hormuz is choking, gold is at $5,300, 10-year yields are surging toward their biggest single-day move since April, and the Fed's rate-cut calendar is being shredded in real time. Everything else this morning is noise.

sentiment.label: Risk-Off
sentiment.context: U.S.-Israeli strikes on Iran, retaliatory missile attacks on Gulf states, Hormuz shipping disruption, and surging oil prices are driving a broad cross-asset risk-off repricing as Monday's U.S. market open approaches.

stories.headline: Iran War Sends Oil Surging, Bonds Tumbling, Dollar Higher as Hormuz Threat Bites
stories.body: West Texas Intermediate settled above $71 as the near-halt of Strait of Hormuz traffic and a Saudi refinery disruption stoked supply fears, while European natural gas soared after Qatar shuttered the world's largest LNG export facility. Ten-year Treasury yields jumped 11 basis points to 4.05% — their biggest single-day advance since April — as inflation fears evaporated Fed rate-cut bets, with markets now pricing the first cut no earlier than September. Gold topped $5,300 and the dollar rose 0.7%, while airlines sank and the S&P 500 clawed back from a 1%-plus intraday loss.

stories.headline: Iran Strikes Dubai, Abu Dhabi, Qatar, and Bahrain in Retaliation for U.S.-Israeli Bombing Campaign
stories.body: Iran widened its retaliatory strike package beyond Israel to target Gulf states hosting U.S. allies and military infrastructure, hitting Dubai, Abu Dhabi, Qatar, and Bahrain. The UAE halted its stock markets for two days following the attacks, a stark signal of how severely the Gulf's image as a stable financial hub has been shaken. Three American service members were confirmed killed, and the U.N. Security Council convened an emergency session with the Secretary-General warning the escalation could spiral into a broader regional conflict.

stories.headline: Bitcoin Holds Near $66,000 Ahead of Monday Open as Hormuz Risk Threatens Macro Repricing
stories.body: Bitcoin printed a weekend low of $63,068 before recovering to roughly $66,999, but the real test arrives Monday when U.S. spot ETF flows and institutional liquidity resume. Elevated energy prices feed directly into inflation expectations and rate pricing, which then shape risk appetite for high-beta assets like BTC — meaning if the Hormuz risk premium persists at the open, Bitcoin is likely to trade as a macro proxy rather than a crypto-specific story. The UN Security Council alarm and continued Iran retaliation increase the odds that Monday becomes a broad cross-asset repricing event rather than a contained weekend shock.

stories.headline: Berkshire Q4 Operating Profit Drops 30% to $10.2B as Insurance Income Collapses and Occidental Written Down
stories.body: Berkshire Hathaway reported a 30% fall in fourth-quarter operating profit to $10.2 billion, driven by a 38% collapse in insurance earnings as falling interest rates pressured cash income and Geico faced pricing constraints. The company also took a $4.5 billion writedown on its 26.9% stake in Occidental Petroleum, its second such charge in 2025, though it stated no intention to sell. New CEO Greg Abel inherits a $373.3 billion cash pile and a business CFRA analyst Cathy Seifert described bluntly: 'Top-line growth is a challenge.'

stories.headline: BYD February Sales Plunge 41% Year-on-Year as Chinese Demand Sputters
stories.body: BYD sold 190,190 vehicles in February, down 41% from a year earlier and 9.5% from January, with the two-month combined figure off 36% at 400,241 units as Lunar New Year disruptions compounded a structural demand softening at home. Reduced purchase-tax exemptions, cooling consumer confidence, and buyers delaying purchases ahead of new model launches are all weighing on domestic volumes. International expansion — particularly in Latin America and Europe — is now carrying the growth story, with exports of new energy vehicles reaching 100,600 units last month.

stories.headline: Iran's Soccer Federation Casts Doubt on 2026 World Cup Participation After U.S.-Israeli Strikes
stories.body: Iranian football federation president Mehdi Taj said flatly that his country 'cannot be expected to look forward to the World Cup with hope' following the strikes that killed Supreme Leader Khamenei. Iran is slated to play Group G matches in Los Angeles and Seattle beginning June 15, but a 40-day national mourning period has halted team preparations and Iranian citizens already face U.S. travel restrictions. FIFA said it is monitoring the situation, which adds a material logistical complication to a tournament the U.S. is co-hosting.

quote.text: Oil is the lifeblood of the industrialized nations. A cutoff of oil imports would bring the industrialized countries to their knees.
